factors.name: DCC_Correlation
factors.description: Time-varying dynamic conditional correlation between asset pairs estimated via DCC-GARCH framework that captures regime-dependent correlation structures

params.a: DCC alpha (shock impact on correlation, typically 0.05-0.10)

l1.definition: Dynamic conditional correlation that evolves over time based on past standardized residuals, capturing correlation regime changes
l1.business_constraints: Requires univariate GARCH estimation first; computationally feasible for N <= 100 assets

calculation_steps.description: Fit univariate GARCH(1,1) to each asset
calculation_steps.formula: σ_i,t² = ω_i + α_i * ε_i,t-1² + β_i * σ_i,t-1²

l2.edge_case_handling: Ensure positive definiteness via Q_bar initialization; use near-benchmark correlation if matrix becomes non-positive definite
l2.missing_value_handling: Listwise deletion within rolling window; require minimum 60% data coverage
l2.complexity: O(T × N²) for N assets and T time periods

l3.financial_intuition: Correlations are not constant but rise during market stress. DCC captures this by making correlations respond to shock innovations while maintaining persistence from historical patterns.
l3.market_behavior: Detects correlation regime shifts: low stable correlations during normal periods, rapid correlation spikes during crises
l3.theoretical_basis: Engle (2002) DCC-GARCH model extending Bollerslev (1990) CCC framework with time-varying correlations
l3.historical_effectiveness: Empirically validated on stocks, bonds, currencies; outperforms constant correlation models in crisis periods
l3.related_factors: Related to implied correlation, VIX, cross-asset correlation metrics; complements volatility factor for portfolio construction

hypotheses.name: Correlation Regime Shift Hypothesis
hypotheses.description: DCC correlations detect regime changes earlier than constant correlation models
hypotheses.source: DCC model captures time-varying nature of correlations

hypotheses.name: Crisis Correlation Spike Hypothesis
hypotheses.description: DCC correlations increase significantly during market stress periods

hypotheses.name: Correlation Mean Reversion Hypothesis
hypotheses.description: DCC correlations revert toward long-term average after shock dissipation

l4.meaning_summary: Captures the dynamic, regime-dependent nature of inter-asset correlations enabling better portfolio risk estimation and dynamic hedging
key_insights: Parsimonious: reduces parameters from O(N^4) to O(N^2 + 2) for N assets
Two-step estimation enables scalability to large asset universes
l4.uncertainty: Parameter sensitivity to estimation window; assumes symmetric response to positive/negative shocks

factors.name: DCC_Alpha_Parameter
factors.description: Speed of adjustment parameter in DCC model controlling how quickly correlations respond to new shock innovations
factors.formula: a ∈ [0, 1), typically estimated via QMLE in range [0.05, 0.10]

signal_params.interpretation: higher alpha = faster correlation response to shocks

l1.definition: DCC model parameter controlling the immediate impact of shock innovations on time-varying correlations
l1.business_constraints: Estimated via maximum likelihood; typically ranges 0.05-0.10 for financial returns

calculation_steps.description: Initialize Q_0 as unconditional correlation of standardized residuals
For each t, compute Q_t using DCC recursion
Maximize log-likelihood with respect to (a, b) parameters

l2.edge_case_handling: Use Box-Cox transformation if likelihood convergence issues arise
l2.missing_value_handling: Full sample maximum likelihood with available data

l3.financial_intuition: Higher alpha indicates correlations react quickly to new information; lower alpha suggests slow, gradual correlation adjustments
l3.market_behavior: Alpha captures correlation 'shock sensitivity' - high alpha markets show rapid correlation transmission
l3.historical_effectiveness: Empirically alpha typically in 0.05-0.10 range for equity returns
l3.related_factors: Complements DCC_Beta for complete DCC specification; related to shock transmission speed in network models

hypotheses.name: Alpha Persistence Tradeoff
hypotheses.description: Higher alpha implies faster correlation adjustment but less persistence

l4.meaning_summary: Measures speed of correlation discovery in response to market shocks
l4.uncertainty: Parameter estimates have standard errors; sensitive to window choice

factors.name: DCC_Beta_Parameter
factors.description: Persistence parameter in DCC model controlling how much past correlation structure influences current correlations

l1.definition: DCC model persistence parameter measuring how much historical correlation patterns influence current correlations
l1.business_constraints: Typically 0.85-0.95 for financial returns; values near 1 indicate slow mean reversion

calculation_steps.formula: Maximize Σ log L(a, b | data)

calculation_steps.description: Compute standard errors via outer product gradient

l2.edge_case_handling: If a + b >= 1, respecify model or reduce window

l3.financial_intuition: Beta near 1 indicates correlations are highly persistent - once elevated, they stay elevated for extended periods
l3.market_behavior: High beta markets show correlation 'clustering' - stress correlations persist even after initial shock
l3.historical_effectiveness: Empirically beta typically 0.85-0.95; higher for bonds than equities
l3.related_factors: Complements DCC_Alpha; related to correlation half-life calculation

hypotheses.name: Correlation Half-Life Hypothesis
hypotheses.description: High beta implies long correlation half-life - regime changes persist

l4.meaning_summary: Measures correlation memory - how long past correlation patterns influence current correlations
l4.uncertainty: Parameter estimates can be unstable in short samples

factors.name: Correlation_Regime_Shift_Indicator
factors.description: Binary or continuous indicator detecting when DCC correlations exceed historical thresholds or show sudden regime transitions
factors.formula: CRS_t = I(R_t^{DCC} > threshold) or CRS_t = (R_t^{DCC} - μ_R) / σ_R

l1.definition: Indicator identifying when dynamic correlations enter stress regimes based on deviation from historical norms
l1.business_constraints: Used for dynamic position sizing; requires sufficient history for threshold calibration

calculation_steps.description: Calculate rolling mean and std of DCC correlations
calculation_steps.formula: μ_R = (1/T) Σ R_t, σ_R = sqrt((1/T) Σ (R_t - μ_R)²)

calculation_steps.description: Classify regime based on z-score thresholds
Generate regime shift signal if transition detected

l2.edge_case_handling: Handle regime persistence: require N consecutive observations before declaring regime change
l2.missing_value_handling: Use expanding window for first lookback periods

l3.financial_intuition: Portfolio diversification fails precisely when needed most - this indicator detects when correlation regime shifts into stress mode
l3.market_behavior: Normal: low stable correlations; Elevated: correlation rising; Stress: correlations spike above threshold
l3.theoretical_basis: Based on DCC model properties and empirical correlation distribution analysis
l3.historical_effectiveness: Effective during 2008 crisis, 2020 COVID crash; identifies correlation breakdown earlier than rolling windows
l3.related_factors: Related to VIX, global risk factors, cross-asset correlation indicators; complements momentum for regime detection

hypotheses.name: Correlation Regime Predictability
hypotheses.description: High DCC correlations predict subsequent return co-movement

hypotheses.name: Diversification Failure Timing
hypotheses.description: Correlation regime shifts precede or coincide with drawdowns

l4.meaning_summary: Detects when inter-asset correlations enter stress regimes, enabling dynamic portfolio rebalancing before diversification fails
key_insights: Crisis correlations often exceed 0.8 for previously uncorrelated assets
Regime persistence matters: brief spikes may be noise
Threshold calibration critical - too sensitive creates false signals
l4.uncertainty: Threshold sensitivity; lag in detection during rapid regime shifts

factors.name: DCC_Composite_Signal
factors.description: Composite signal combining DCC correlations with volatility measures for portfolio optimization and risk management applications
factors.formula: Signal_t = w_1 * CRS_t + w_2 * DCC_t + w_3 * σ_t

l1.definition: Multi-factor composite signal integrating DCC correlation dynamics, regime shift indicators, and volatility measures for cross-asset portfolio optimization
l1.business_constraints: Used for dynamic position sizing; requires DCC estimation completed first; computationally feasible for N <= 100

calculation_steps.formula: raw_signal = w_1 * z_regime + w_2 * z_corr + w_3 * z_vol

l2.edge_case_handling: Cap signal at ±2σ to limit extreme positions; require minimum 60% data coverage
l2.missing_value_handling: Use last available values with decay for up to 5 days

l3.financial_intuition: Combines correlation regime detection with volatility information for optimal risk-adjusted position sizing across multiple assets
l3.market_behavior: In normal markets: lower correlations favor diversification; in stress: reduce exposure due to elevated correlations
l3.theoretical_basis: DCC-GARCH for correlations; Markowitz optimization for portfolio construction
l3.historical_effectiveness: Composite signal improves Sharpe ratio vs single-factor approaches; reduces drawdown during correlation spikes
l3.related_factors: Combines DCC_Correlation, Correlation_Regime_Shift_Indicator, GARCH_volatility into unified framework

hypotheses.name: Dynamic Correlation Advantage
hypotheses.description: Composite signal using DCC correlations outperforms static correlation portfolios

hypotheses.name: Regime-Based Position Sizing
hypotheses.description: Adjusting positions based on correlation regime improves risk-adjusted returns

l4.meaning_summary: Unified signal combining correlation dynamics, regime detection, and volatility for sophisticated multi-asset portfolio management
key_insights: DCC captures time-varying correlations missed by constant models
Regime indicator provides actionable threshold signals
Composite approach balances multiple risk dimensions
l4.uncertainty: Weight optimization challenging; requires backtesting for parameter tuning

reasoning: DCC_Correlation is selected as primary because it is the core output of Engle (2002)'s DCC-GARCH framework and directly captures the time-varying correlation dynamics that distinguish this approach from constant correlation models. The other factors (alpha/beta parameters, regime indicator, composite signal) are derived from or depend on DCC_Correlation. The volatility signal type is appropriate as DCC models capture both volatility clustering and correlation dynamics, making it suitable for risk management and portfolio optimization applications.
